content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcal PnacrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-A886
April 15,2005
Elizabeth Hull Wagner
City of Chula Vista
276 Fourth Avenue
ChulaVista, CA 91910
Re
Your Request for fnformal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-05-058
Dear Ms. Wagner:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of the City of Chula Vista
Councilmember John McCann for advice regmding the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act')-l Because no facts are provided r"gu.dirg yo.r.
sources of income, we provide you informal assistance.2
QUESTION
May Councilmember McCarm participate in govemmental decisions affectingreal
property in which he no longer orilns an economic interest?
CONCLUSION
Councilmember McCann no longer holds an interest in real property that would
be affected by upcoming city council decisions. Therefore, he will be able to participate
in the decisions so long as the decisions will not have a material financial effect on the
purchasers of the property or tenants.
' Gore--".rt Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 14; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).


File No. I-05-058
Page No. 2
FACTS
In December 2003, our office issued a letter advising that without additional
information it appeared that Councilmernber McCann was unable to participate in a
decision regarding the hiring and funding of a consultant to prepare an Urban Core
Specific Plan ("UCSP'). Since this time, the Community Development Department of
the City of Chula Vista has continued to process the UCSP. The UCSP will provide a
neighborhood level plan to effectively implement the goals and policies for the City's
urban core as identified in the General Plan. The UCSP will address, at the neighborhood
level, Iand use mix and diskibution, zoningurban and architectural design, vehicular and
pedestrian circulation, parking, transit services and facilities, public improvements, and
implementation procedures.
In Decernber 2W3, CouncilmemberMcCann owned a rental property located
within 500 feet ofthe UCSP area. Theproperty as a rental was a source of income to
him, as was the tenant. Based upon the letter you received from us in December 2003,
Councilmember McCann has not participated in decisions regarding the UCSP- In
January 2005, Councilmernber McCann listed the property for sale. Shortly thereafter,
the property was placed in escrow and as of February 23,2OA5, Councilmernber McCann
no longer owns the property. The sale was an arms length transaction and the property
sold at its fairmarket value. CouncilmernberMcCann has no ongoing interest in the
property.
ANALYSIS
Your inquiry implicates the Act's conflict ofinterest rules, sections 87100 et seq-
In applying section 87103, the Commission employs an eight step analytical framework
to assist in determining whether a public official has a disqualifinng conflict of interest.
(Regulation 1870qb) (1) - O) (8).) We review your circumstances within this eight-step
framework.
Steps l. &2. Is the indirridual a public official and will that individual be making
participating in making or affempting to influence a government decision?
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making participating
in making or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has an economic interest- A "public official" is defined in
section 82C/;8 and regulation 18701 as every natmal person who is a mernber, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local government agency. Councilmember McCann
is a "public official" and wishes to make decisions involving the planning and
development of the USCP area.
Step 3. What are the official's economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from
economic interests. A public official has an economic interest in:


File No. I-05-058
Page No. 3
a
A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect3 investment of $2,000 or
more (Section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee or holds any position of management (Section
871 03(d); regulation 187 03.1 O));
Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more
(Section 871 03(b); regulation 187 03.2);
Any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more
within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3);
Any source of gifts to him orher if the gifts aggregate to $360 ormore within 12
months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); regulation $7A3.$;
o His or her personal finances, includilrg those of his or her immediate familya -- this is
the "personal financial effects" rule.' (Section 87103; regulation r 8703.5.)
Based on the facts you presented to us in your letter of March zl, zo05,
Councilmernber McCann no longer has real property located within 500 feet of the
UCSP. However, the council mernber continues to have two economic interests that may
be affected by the decision in question. First, his tenant is considered a source of income
to the council member for 12 months after the last rental payment was received by him.
Second, the purchaser of the property is also considered a source of income assuming that
the source paid $500 or more to the council memb€r- The purchaser will continue to be a
source of income for 12 months after the date the sale was consummated --Febraary 23,
2005.
Step 4. Are the economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?
Under regulation 18704.1(a), a souroe of income is directly involved in a decision
before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will bemade by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
3 For putposes of section 87103,'fudirect investnent or interest means any inveshent or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or kust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own
directly, indirectly, or beneficially a lO-percent interest or greater.',
" A public official's "immediate family''includes only the official's spouse and dependent
children. (Section 82029.)
5 In addition to the economic interests separately listed in section 87103, a public official has an
economic interest in his or her personal finances, and may have a conflict ofinterest in any decision
foreseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities ofthe
official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a l2-month period.
o
a
a


File No. I-05-058
Page No. 4
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning
the decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement
to, or contract with, the subject person."
Neither of the sources of income identified in your letter me "directly involved"
in the decision as defined in regulation 187M.2. Thus, they are considered indirectly
involved in the decision.
Steps 5. & 6. What are the applicable materiality standards and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effects of the governmental decisions on your economic
interests will meet this materiality standard?
If a source of income is not directly involved in the decision, then the materiality
standards of regulation 18705.3(b)(3) would apply.6 Regulation 18705.30)(3) provides:
'"The effect of a decision is material as to an individual who is a source of
income to an official if any of the following applies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's incomg investnents, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property)
by $1,000 ormore; or
"(B) The decision will affect theindividual's real propertyinterest in a
manner that is considered material under Title2, Califomia Code of
Regulations, sections I 87 05.2(b)."
Regulation I 8705.20) states:
"The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which
is indirectly involved in the governmental decision is presrmred not to be
material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are
specific circumstances regarding the governmental decisiorl its financial
effect, and the nature of real property in which the public official has an
economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect on the real property in which the
public official has an interest. Examples of specific circumstances that will
be considered include, but are not limited to, circumstances where the
decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
6 We assume the purchaser of the property and the tenants are individuals. Ifthey are entities, the
provisions ofregulation 1 8705.3(b)(1) and (b[2) apply (copy enclosed).


File No. I-05-058
Page No. 5
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity ofuse, noise levels,
air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."
Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is deterrnining whether it is "reasonably
foreseeable" thatthe standard will bemet. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered'?easonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a merepossibility. (In re rhorner (1975) I FPPC ops. 198.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra.)
Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In re
Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), the foreseeability of aparticular financial effect is a
determination that must be left to the public official.
Steps 7. & 8. The (public generally'and *Iegally required participation,
exceptions.
Step seven applies an exception when the reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally. (Section 83112; regulation 15707.) Step eight applies an exception
when the official's participation in a goveflrmental decision is legally required. (Section
87101; regulation 18708-) Both of these narrowly construed exceptions are fact-driven,
and you have provided no facts indicating that these exceptions apply here.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: Sukhdip Brar
Intem, Legal Division
Enclosures

18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 14; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmember McCarm participate in govemmental decisions affectingreal
property in which he no longer orilns an economic interest?

CONCLUSION: Councilmember McCann no longer holds an interest in real property that would
be affected by upcoming city council decisions. Therefore, he will be able to participate
in the decisions so long as the decisions will not have a material financial effect on the
purchasers of the property or tenants.
' Gore--".rt Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 14; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).
